content.text: When your application already runs a NoSQL database — Redis for caching, MongoDB Atlas for documents, Cassandra for high-write workloads, or DynamoDB for serverless AWS services — you can bolt vector search onto the same infrastructure rather than adding a separate vector database. Each platform has taken a different approach to integrating approximate nearest-neighbour (ANN) search with its native data model.

This sub-subtopic covers Redis Stack, MongoDB Atlas Vector Search, Cassandra (DataStax Astra), and the DynamoDB + OpenSearch pattern: when each is the right choice, how to store and query embeddings, and what the operational trade-offs are.

attrs.alt: NoSQL vector store decision map: Redis Stack, MongoDB Atlas, Cassandra/Astra, DynamoDB+OpenSearch — use cases and capabilities

content.text: Redis Stack ships two modules that together provide vector search:
stores HNSW indexes entirely in RAM, and

stores the document payload as structured JSON. Combined, they support sub-millisecond ANN queries, making Redis the right choice when latency is the primary constraint and your dataset fits in available memory.

import redis
import json, struct

r = redis.Redis(host="localhost", port=6379, decode_responses=False)

def store_chunk(doc_id: str, content: str, embedding: list[float], tenant_id: str) -> None:
    key = f"chunk:{doc_id}"
    # Store payload as JSON (RedisJSON)
    r.json().set(key, "$", {
        "content": content,
        "tenant_id": tenant_id,
    })
    # Add the vector blob (32-bit floats, little-endian)
    vec_bytes = struct.pack(f"{len(embedding)}f", *embedding)
    r.hset(key, mapping={"embedding": vec_bytes})
Create the HNSW index once at startup (or on schema change):

from redis.commands.search.query import Query
import struct

def search_redis(
    query_embedding: list[float],
    tenant_id: str,
    top_k: int = 10,
) -> list[dict]:
    vec_bytes = struct.pack(f"{len(query_embedding)}f", *query_embedding)

    q = (
        Query(f"(@tenant_id:{{{tenant_id}}})=>[KNN {top_k} @embedding $vec AS score]")
        .sort_by("score")
        .return_fields("$.content", "score", "tenant_id")
        .dialect(2)
    )
    results = r.ft("chunks_idx").search(q, query_params={"vec": vec_bytes})
    return [
        {"content": doc["$.content"], "score": float(doc["score"])}
        for doc in results.docs
    ]

each FLOAT32 vector of 1,536 dimensions uses 6 KB. One million chunks consume ≈ 6 GB just for embeddings. Add HNSW graph overhead (≈ 30–50% extra) and RedisJSON payload. Size your Redis instance accordingly, or enable
storage (Redis 7.4+) to spill cold entries to disk.
Atlas Vector Search adds a managed HNSW index directly onto BSON documents. Queries use the
aggregation stage, which integrates naturally with
stages. This makes it a natural fit for applications already storing structured content in Atlas collections.
Creating a vector search index (Atlas Data API)
Define the index via the Atlas UI or the Atlas CLI. Equivalent JSON definition:

from pymongo import MongoClient
from datetime import datetime, timezone

client = MongoClient("mongodb+srv://<user>:<pass>@cluster.mongodb.net/")
db = client["myapp"]
chunks = db["chunks"]

async def upsert_chunk_mongo(
    doc_id: str,
    chunk_index: int,
    content: str,
    embedding: list[float],
    tenant_id: str,
    lang: str = "en",
) -> None:
    chunks.update_one(
        {"doc_id": doc_id, "chunk_index": chunk_index},
        {"$set": {
            "doc_id": doc_id,
            "chunk_index": chunk_index,
            "content": content,
            "embedding": embedding,
            "tenant_id": tenant_id,
            "lang": lang,
            "updated_at": datetime.now(timezone.utc),
        }},
        upsert=True,
    )
field controls the ANN search breadth: 10×
is the Atlas recommendation for good recall under filter conditions. Raising it improves recall but increases latency.
column support with Storage-Attached Indexes (SAI). DataStax Astra DB is the managed cloud offering. CQL (Cassandra Query Language) uses the
clause for vector search, combined with regular CQL predicates.

CREATE KEYSPACE IF NOT EXISTS rag
    WITH replication = {'class': 'NetworkTopologyStrategy', 'datacenter1': 3};

USE rag;

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS chunks (
    tenant_id  UUID,
    doc_id     UUID,
    chunk_idx  INT,
    content    TEXT,
    embedding  VECTOR<FLOAT, 1536>,
    lang       TEXT,
    created_at TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY ((tenant_id, doc_id), chunk_idx)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (chunk_idx ASC);

CREATE CUSTOM INDEX IF NOT EXISTS chunks_embedding_idx
    ON chunks (embedding)
    USING 'StorageAttachedIndex'
    WITH OPTIONS = {'similarity_function': 'cosine'};

from cassandra.cluster import Cluster
from cassandra.auth import PlainTextAuthProvider

auth = PlainTextAuthProvider("token", "<ASTRA_DB_TOKEN>")
cluster = Cluster(cloud={"secure_connect_bundle": "secure-connect.zip"}, auth_provider=auth)
session = cluster.connect("rag")

def upsert_chunk_cassandra(tenant_id, doc_id, chunk_idx, content, embedding, lang="en"):
    session.execute(
        """INSERT INTO chunks (tenant_id, doc_id, chunk_idx, content, embedding, lang, created_at)
           VALUES (%s, %s, %s, %s, %s, %s, toTimestamp(now()))""",
        (tenant_id, doc_id, chunk_idx, content, embedding, lang),
    )

def search_cassandra(
    query_embedding: list[float],
    tenant_id: str,
    top_k: int = 10,
) -> list[dict]:
    rows = session.execute(
        f"""SELECT content, doc_id, similarity_cosine(embedding, %s) AS score
              FROM chunks
              WHERE tenant_id = %s
              ORDER BY embedding ANN OF %s
              LIMIT %s""",
        (query_embedding, tenant_id, query_embedding, top_k),
    )
    return [{"content": r.content, "doc_id": str(r.doc_id), "score": r.score} for r in rows]

as part of the partition key means all chunks for the same tenant + document land on the same replica set. This co-location makes the ANN scan efficient but means extremely large tenants create hot partitions. For very large tenants add a

DynamoDB itself has no vector index. The standard pattern is to use DynamoDB as the primary key-value store (for writes, lookups, and ACID semantics) and stream inserts to OpenSearch Serverless via DynamoDB Streams + Lambda. OpenSearch handles ANN queries.

# Lambda handler triggered by DynamoDB Stream
import json, boto3, struct

oss = boto3.client("opensearchserverless", region_name="us-east-1")
INDEX = "chunks"

def handler(event, context):
    for record in event["Records"]:
        if record["eventName"] not in ("INSERT", "MODIFY"):
            continue
        new = record["dynamodb"]["NewImage"]
        doc = {
            "tenant_id": new["tenant_id"]["S"],
            "content":   new["content"]["S"],
            "embedding": [float(v) for v in new["embedding"]["L"]],
        }
        chunk_id = new["id"]["S"]
        # Index into OpenSearch
        oss.index(
            index=INDEX,
            id=chunk_id,
            body=json.dumps(doc),
        )
Redis HNSW and OpenSearch kNN indexes load into RAM at node start. Plan for warm-up time before routing production traffic after a restart.
all four platforms require dropping and rebuilding the vector index if the embedding dimension changes. Keep the dimension as a config constant and treat it as a breaking schema change.

Redis is eventually consistent across replicas (Redis Sentinel / Cluster). DynamoDB Streams introduces async delay to OpenSearch (typically < 1 s). If you need read-your-writes after upsert, add a short delay or use a two-phase approach.
Redis charges for RAM; Atlas and OpenSearch Serverless charge for index and query compute. Profile your embedding count and query rate before committing to a tier.
when you need sub-millisecond latency and your embedding dataset fits in memory — ideal for caching-heavy RAG or real-time retrieval.
when your application already stores rich BSON documents in Atlas and you want to combine relational-style filters with ANN in a single aggregation pipeline.
when you need globally distributed, write-heavy ingestion with multi-region replication and partition-level ANN isolation.
when you are fully on AWS, need serverless scaling, and can tolerate the async fan-out latency from Streams to the search index.
All four platforms require explicit index rebuild on dimension change and careful RAM / compute cost planning proportional to embedding count.
